title: Carved birch kuksa
subtitle: A single-piece drinking cup in the Sámi tradition
excerpt: A carved birch kuksa in the Sámi single-piece tradition. Green birch burl or branch section, hook-knife hollowing, raw-linseed cure. An intermediate project that builds hook-knife control and teaches inside-curve reading.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for tool progression and hollowing technique. The kuksa form documented in Scandinavian folk-craft archives; construction method consistent with descriptions in Newey & Drage Practical Carpentry and the Museum of English Rural Life open-access collection.

glossaryTerms.definition: A single-piece carved drinking cup from the Sámi tradition, usually birch, with a thick-walled body, flat base, and loop handle carved from the same piece.
Freshly felled or recently riven wood, still high in moisture content. Carves easily under hand tools.
A short length of round or split timber, ready to be worked into a blank.
The rough-shaped piece of wood from which the finished item is carved.
A curved-bladed knife for hollowing the interior of a spoon bowl or kuksa cup. Comes in small, medium, large, and deep variants.
A leather strap charged with abrasive compound, used to maintain a sharp edge between sharpenings.

content.text: Cutting tools are sharp. Work with the blade travelling away from your body and your free hand behind the cutting edge at all times. Strop the tool before starting and whenever the edge feels rough.
A
kuksa
is a single-piece drinking cup carved from a short section of
green

birch. The wet timber carves under hand tools with much less effort than a seasoned piece. Birch is the traditional choice: close-grained, food-safe once oiled, and widely available. The project is intermediate because the interior hollowing requires a deep
hook knife
and the ability to read the inside curve of the cup wall as you work.
The wood
You need a green birch
billet

roughly 15 cm long and 10 cm in diameter. A straight-grained section of branch works well. Burl (the knotty growth lumps at branch junctions) gives a more interesting grain pattern but makes hollowing harder. For a first kuksa, use straight-grained branch section.

Birch carves cleanly green and dries food-safe pale. Silver birch and downy birch both work. If birch is unavailable, sycamore is the next best option: similar working properties, also food-safe, slightly softer. Avoid oak (too hard green), pine (resinous), and any treated or composite timber.

The cut surface should darken within seconds and the chips should fly clean. If the chip tears and clings, the wood is drying out. Work quickly once the billet is off the tree, or wrap it in a damp cloth between sessions.
Tools

The essential kit: a carving axe for rough-shaping the outer profile, a sloyd knife for refining the outside and carving the handle, and a deep hook knife for the interior. A brace and bit bores the pilot hole that starts the hollowing. A
strop
is non-negotiable. A medium hook knife is useful for refining the rim once the deep knife has done the main hollowing.
Workspace

A low stool or a carver's seat at knee height. The workpiece rests on your thigh for axe work; for knife and hook-knife work, you hold it in your free hand. Good light is important for reading the inside curve of the cup wall. Keep the floor under your feet clear.
Shaping the blank

Start with the billet flat-side down on your thigh. Use push cuts with the carving axe to rough out the outside profile: a rounded body, a flat base, and a stub that will become the handle. Work one face at a time, taking slices with the axe rather than full chopping blows. The grain runs along the length of the billet; cuts that travel from the centre of the cup toward the rim go with the grain and cut cleanly. Cuts that travel from rim toward centre go against the grain and the axe catches.

Once the axe has given you the approximate shape, switch to the sloyd knife. Use push cuts and thumb-pivot cuts to refine the outer profile. The base should be flat enough to sit without rocking. The body walls should be roughly 12 mm thick at this stage; you will thin them from the inside.
Boring the pilot hole
Clamp or hold the
blank

firmly. With the brace and bit, bore a hole roughly 20 mm in diameter straight down into the top face, stopping 15 mm short of the base. This pilot hole gives the hook knife somewhere to start its cut. Without it, the hook knife has no entry point and the first cuts are slow and awkward.
Hollowing the interior

Hold the blank in your non-knife hand with the opening facing you. Insert the deep hook knife into the pilot hole. The hook cuts on the pull stroke. Draw the knife toward you in short arcs, working from the centre of the base outward and upward toward the rim. Each stroke lifts a small chip.

Work slowly around the inside circumference, always pulling the knife away from your body and never across your free hand. The inside of the cup wall should become gradually thinner and more even. Hold the blank up to the light periodically; you can see through thin spots as translucent patches. If a patch goes translucent, stop hollowing that area and work on the thick areas instead.
The finished wall thickness is 5 to 7 mm. The base is left slightly thicker at 8 mm. Strop every fifteen minutes. A dull hook knife drags rather than cuts and is far harder to control.
Carving the handle

The handle stub is shaped with the sloyd knife using push cuts. The classic kuksa handle has a loop shape: a hole bored or carved through it so the cup can hang from a belt or pack strap. Bore the loop hole with the brace and bit, then clean up the inside of the loop with the sloyd knife using a series of small thumb-pivot cuts.

The handle outer surface is rounded and smoothed with push cuts. The grain in the handle runs parallel to the length; cuts that travel along the grain lift clean shavings. Cuts across the grain can chip.
Drying

A green birch kuksa will crack if it dries too fast. Leave it in a cool place out of direct sun and away from heat sources for two to three weeks before finishing. Wrap it in a paper bag, not a plastic one. If small checks (hairline cracks) appear at the rim, that is normal; deep cracks from rapid drying are not.
Finishing

Once the kuksa is dry, sand the interior and exterior lightly with 400-grit sandpaper. Apply raw linseed oil liberally: pour a good amount into the cup interior, rub it into the outside, and let it soak for one hour. Wipe off all the surplus. The oil needs two full weeks to cure before the cup is food-safe. Do not use the cup for drinking before that.

Walnut oil and pure tung oil are alternatives. Both cure harder than raw linseed. Walnut oil carries a tree-nut allergen note; declare this if the cup is a gift. Pure tung oil takes three to four weeks to fully set.
Variations
Larger kuksa for a substantial mug: start with a billet 18 cm long and 14 cm across. Same method; longer at each stage.
Sycamore in place of birch: the same working properties, slightly softer, dries to a cream rather than white. Also food-safe.
Left-handed handle: the loop curves to the right on a right-handed kuksa. Mirror the handle stub for a left-handed cup so the grip falls naturally.
Care

Wash the kuksa by hand with warm water and mild soap, then towel-dry immediately. Never soak, never put in the dishwasher. Re-oil with raw linseed or walnut oil two or three times a year, or whenever the wood looks dry.

rows.symptom: Interior walls went translucent during hollowing
rows.cause: Hook knife removed too much wood in one area
rows.fix: Stop hollowing that section. Work on the thick areas until the wall is even. Translucent patches 4 mm or under are still safe; thinner than that risks a hole.

rows.symptom: Hook knife drags and chatters rather than cutting
rows.fix: Strop immediately. Ten passes on a charged leather strop should restore the edge. If stropping does not help, hone on the 1000-grit waterstone.

rows.cause: Dried too fast, usually in direct sun or near a heat source
rows.fix: Small checks are cosmetic. Deepen the check with a thin blade and pack with a wood glue and sawdust paste, then re-oil when dry. For large cracks, the cup is structural only (no liquid contact) unless the crack is away from the body walls.

rows.symptom: Sloyd knife catches and tears on handle grain
rows.cause: Cutting against the grain on the handle stub
rows.fix: Reverse the cut direction. Cuts that travel away from the body toward the handle tip go with the grain; cuts that travel back toward the body go against it.
